messages.content: random thing i've always wondered, why do we get hiccups? like what's the actual point of them

The honest answer is that nobody's quite sure — hiccups seem to be a leftover reflex with no clear purpose in adult humans.

The leading theory is that it's an ancient mechanism inherited from gill-breathing ancestors, where a similar reflex helped clear water from the airway. In us, it just fires occasionally when the diaphragm gets irritated — by eating too fast, carbonation, sudden temperature changes.

What made you think of that today?
my niece had them forever yesterday and it got me curious about the whole thing

That tracks — there's something about watching someone else stuck in a hiccup loop that makes you really notice how absurd the whole thing is.

How old is she?
i swear everyone's got their own folk cure too. my grandma always did the spoonful of sugar thing

The sugar one actually has some logic behind it — the theory is it stimulates the vagus nerve, which can reset the diaphragm's rhythm. Whether it works or it's just distraction is hard to say.

What's your go-to when they hit you?
it's kinda funny how something so common is apparently still a bit of a mystery science-wise

It really is — and hiccups aren't alone in that. Yawning, why we dream, why we laugh — all common, all still genuinely murky.

There's something almost reassuring about that, that the ordinary stuff can still be unresolved.
